timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Remnant Flees to Egypt

sections.header: Verses 1-6 -- "Pray to the LORD for Us"

heb.paragraph: The people invoke God as witness ('ed) to their oath: "May the LORD be a true and faithful witness ('ed) against us if we do not act in accordance with everything the LORD your God sends you to tell us" (v.5). The oath is solemn and comprehensive: "Whether it is favorable or unfavorable, we will obey" (v.6). They will break this oath within days.

refs.note: "Speak, LORD, for your servant is listening" -- the model of genuine receptivity that the remnant claims but does not practice.
"Do not merely listen to the word -- do what it says" -- the NT warning against the gap between hearing and obeying.

notes.note: Johanan and the people ask Jeremiah to pray for guidance. The request implies they are still open to staying in the land. But their preparations for Egypt (41:17) suggest the decision may already be made.
The oath: "May the LORD be a true and faithful witness against us." They invoke their own condemnation if they disobey -- and then proceed to disobey.

Calvin warns against "performative obedience" -- the appearance of submission without the substance. "Many ask for God's guidance with their mouths while their feet are already moving in the opposite direction."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase "may our petition come before you" (tippol-na' tehinnatenu lephaneka) uses the verb naphal ("to fall") -- literally "let our petition fall before you," a posture of prostration.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as "the last formal consultation with the prophet." The pattern repeats from Zedekiah's consultations (37:17, 38:14): ask the prophet, receive unwanted truth, reject it.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the oath as "self-deceiving sincerity." The people genuinely believe they will obey -- but only because they assume God will tell them what they already want to hear.

hist.context: The remnant approaches Jeremiah at Geruth Kimham and asks him to pray for divine guidance about whether to go to Egypt. Their oath is elaborate and apparently sincere: they will obey whatever God says. Jeremiah agrees and waits ten days for the word. The delay itself is a test of patience -- and a test of whether the decision to flee to Egypt has already been made in their hearts.

heb.paragraph: The divine answer is emphatic: "Do not ('al) go to Egypt" (v.19). The negative particle 'al with the jussive expresses strong prohibition. God offers a clear choice: stay in the land and be restored (vv.10-12), or go to Egypt and face the sword, famine, and plague you are running from (vv.15-17). The warning is absolute: "not one of them will survive" (v.17).

refs.note: "To uproot and tear down, to build and to plant" -- the call's vocabulary now applied positively for the first time to this remnant.
"The LORD will send you back in ships to Egypt" -- the covenant curse of returning to bondage.

notes.note: Ten days of waiting -- a test of patience. The delay separates genuine seekers from those who have already decided.
The conditional promise: stay and be built up. "Do not be afraid of the king of Babylon" -- the same fear that drove Zedekiah now threatens the remnant.
The warning: in Egypt, "the sword and famine you dread will follow you there." Running from judgment does not outrun God.

Jeremiah makes it personal: "I have told you today, but you still have not obeyed." He names the sin before it happens -- they made a "fatal mistake" (v.20) when they asked for guidance they never intended to follow.
Calvin highlights the tenderness: "God says 'I relent concerning the disaster.' Even after the fall, God is willing to reverse course if the people will trust him. Grace persists beyond catastrophe."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb niham ("I relent/am sorry") is the same used in 18:8 -- God changes his announced course in response to changed human behavior. The conditional principle from the potter's house still operates.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads the ten-day delay as "prophetic integrity." Jeremiah does not manufacture an instant answer but waits for the genuine divine word. The delay itself distinguishes true from false prophecy.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies the "geography of futility" -- running to Egypt cannot outpace divine judgment. "The illusion that a change of location solves a crisis of faith is as old as Jonah and as contemporary as the modern refugee."

hist.context: After ten days of waiting, God's answer arrives: stay in the land. The promise is rich: "I will build you up and not tear you down; I will plant you and not uproot you" (v.10) -- the constructive verbs from 1:10. God will make Nebuchadnezzar "have compassion" on the remnant (v.12). But if they go to Egypt, the very disasters they flee will follow them there. The irony is precise: Egypt will provide none of the safety they seek.

themes.note: Judgment and Sovereignty at 8 -- the clear word rejected. Mercy at 6: God still offers restoration even after the fall. Faith at 4: the people's oath is hollow.

rows.label: vv.7-12
rows.text: God's Answer: Stay and Be Restored

lit.note: The last formal consultation follows the pattern: ask, receive unwanted truth, reject it. The oath of obedience (vv.5-6) makes the subsequent disobedience even more damning.

hebtext.note: The people invoke God as witness ('ed) to their oath: "May the LORD be a true and faithful witness ('ed) against us if we do not act in accordance with everything the LORD your God sends you to tell us" (v.5). The oath is solemn and comprehensive: "Whether it is favorable or unfavorable, we will obey" (v.6). They will break this oath within days.

The divine answer is emphatic: "Do not ('al) go to Egypt" (v.19). The negative particle 'al with the jussive expresses strong prohibition. God offers a clear choice: stay in the land and be restored (vv.10-12), or go to Egypt and face the sword, famine, and plague you are running from (vv.15-17). The warning is absolute: "not one of them will survive" (v.17).

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Remnant Asks for Guidance

translations.text: “I have heard you,” replied Jeremiah the prophet. “I will certainly pray to the Lord your God as you have requested; I will tell you everything the Lord says and will keep nothing back from you.”

Jeremiah the prophet said to them, “I have heard you. Behold, I will pray to the LORD your God according to your request, and whatever the LORD answers you I will tell you. I will keep nothing back from you.”
Then all the army officers, including Johanan son of Kareah and Jezaniah son of Hoshaiah, and all the people from the least to the greatest approached
Then all the commanders of the forces, and Johanan the son of Kareah and Jezaniah the son of Hoshaiah, and all the people from the least to the greatest, came near

thread.text: "Speak, LORD, for your servant is listening" -- the model of genuine receptivity that the remnant claims but does not practice.
"Do not merely listen to the word -- do what it says" -- the NT warning against the gap between hearing and obeying.
"To uproot and tear down, to build and to plant" -- the call's vocabulary now applied positively for the first time to this remnant.
"The LORD will send you back in ships to Egypt" -- the covenant curse of returning to bondage.

coaching.tip: The remnant swears to obey whatever God says: 'Whether it is favorable or unfavorable, we will obey' (v. 6). The language sounds like covenant renewal — total commitment. But their response in chapter 43 reveals this was conditional obedience: they'll obey only if God says what they already want to hear. The gap between promise and performance is Jeremiah's recurring theme.

God's answer takes ten days (v. 7). The delay tests sincerity — have they already decided before asking? The content confirms their fear: 'Do not go to Egypt' (v. 19). God's answer contradicts their plan, which is exactly the test. Genuine prayer surrenders the outcome; strategic prayer seeks divine endorsement of a predetermined choice. Jeremiah's audience fails the test.
